Still too many `Any`s and castings.

The lack of recursive type checking is annoying, but going down one or two levels in your type hints makes a big difference to code quality and understability. 

For inspiration, see what we did for `cwltool` https://github.com/common-workflow-language/cwltool/blob/7094ede917c2d5b16d11f9231fe0c05260b51be6/cwltool/utils.py#L85

Nice! Glad to have this feature in. Had some brief comments on the docs bit, and I also wanted to propose `output_required` as an alternate name for this parameter.

`always_materialize` seems slightly confusing, just because it isn't immediately clear to me what scope of situations "always" applies to. I also think `output_required` implies a bit more of what the user's workflow will look like (optionally emitting an output).

Open to other suggestions as well.

FYI @illicitonion - when first adding the Target API, you were hoping to ban depending on a `python_binary`. This PR opens up a very uncharted world. I think this use case is sensible, and it's very helpful for an end-user, but brave new world.

Possible sketch of the semantics:

* binary depends on binary -> would be like depending on a library, you'll get the `sources` and `dependencies`, but nothing special.
* test depends on binary -> will both act like depending on a library, but also this special logic.
* library depends on binary -> will be like depending on a library.

Note, Benjy, that this implementation means the `sources` and `dependencies` for that `python_binary` target will still get populated in the chroot for the test, in addition to including the built binary assets. Possibly, we'd only want the built assets? But that would mean having to awkwardly filter out all binary sources from things like `await Get(PythonSources, PythonSourcesRequest)`, which doesn't seem desirable.

What's missing for me in general are unittests for the new operations in `tests/test_transactions.py`. I think you wrote some of those in the past. Either way, this is how I write them usually:

1. Copy&Paste an existing method
2. Change the operation and fill in some random data
3. replace `doit` with `doit(True)` which dumps the generated hex
4. Take the hex and put it into `self.cm`
5. Run the test.

As you can see in `doit()` it compares 

* the generated hex against the hex in the unittest
* the unittest hex against the RPC hex
* the generated hex against the RPC hex

Thanks @matt-boris for looking into this. This seems fine to me, but is also a bit weird.

If I go to Firefox-CI, I get two options:

![Screen Shot 2022-01-25 at 9 17 34 AM](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/286017/151004539-15859834-2bc1-4a0f-879d-7b868af6ff26.png)

If I chose "Sign in with Auth0", then I go through Mozilla's SSO workflow in a new tab. The tab auto-closes, so I can't see if it calls `/login/oauth/authorize`. But maybe this is the one getting rate limited. The `POST` is done in the user's browser, so it would be associated with the user's IP (used by [express-rate-limit](https://github.com/nfriedly/express-rate-limit)).

If you chose "Sign in with credentials", you enter a Client ID and Access Token. These are turned into an authentication headers, and you do not `POST` to `/login/oauth/authorize`. The token is checked when used.

If you sign in on community-tc, the option is "Sign In with Github". In a similar way, a tab is opened, and I suspect it calls `POST` to `/login/oauth/authorize` before closing. Maybe this could be confirmed in the server logs.

So, the potential negative is that valid users will log in with SSO or GitHub, and get rate limited on the final step of the OAuth workflow. If the users are using a Proxy, this is more likely to happen. I think our corporate VPN or other VPNs will look like a proxy, so employees are more likely to see this issue. There are settings to [trust one or more levels of proxy](https://github.com/nfriedly/express-rate-limit#troubleshooting-proxy-issues) to make this less likely. Still, 200 in one minute is a lot!

This mitigation will rate limit requests pretending to be the final steps of an OAuth workflow. Or valid final steps of an OAuth workflow. It does not rate limit:

* Guessing the access tokens for static client IDs
* Guessing OAuth credentials

In other applications, I would add a report of 429 rate limits seen in a day, to see if this worked at all (or negatively impacted actual customs). Maybe we add that as a TODO item for the monitoring project.

one possible source of conflict is if inconsistent case is used for the header key.

e.g. `X-Api-Proxy` vs `x-api-proxy`. `requests` will dedupe the header, but may result in inconsistent behaviour in python2 if an OrderedDict is not being used for the `extra_http_headers` vs `additional_headers`.

This isn't necessarily an issue in Python3 because underlying changes to the builtin dict has made it a de facto ordered-dict as a consequence of its implementation.

Can't we instead create an `ast.Visitor`?

Something like:
```py
from _ast import AnnAssign, Expr
from typing import Any
from pydantic import BaseModel
import inspect
import ast


class Model(BaseModel):
    a: int
    """My docstring."""
    b: int = 1
    """Docstring"""
    c: int = 1
    # This doesn't get to the AST.


class Visitor(ast.NodeVisitor):
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        super().__init__()

        self.target: str | None = None
        self.attrs: dict[str, str] = {}

    def visit_AnnAssign(self, node: AnnAssign) -> Any:
        if isinstance(node.target, ast.Name):
            self.target = node.target.id

    def visit_Expr(self, node: Expr) -> Any:
        if isinstance(node.value, ast.Str):
            docstring = inspect.cleandoc(node.value.s)
            if self.target:
                self.attrs[self.target] = docstring
            self.target = None


source = inspect.getsource(Model)
tree = ast.parse(source)

visitor = Visitor()
visitor.visit(tree)
print(visitor.attrs)
```

This looks like a great start. I have a few thoughts about the design.

If you added a `controller` section to [kubernetes.yaml](https://github.com/dask/dask-kubernetes/blob/main/dask_kubernetes/kubernetes.yaml) we could use the Dask config system instead of using global constants.

```yaml
kubernetes:
  ...
  controller:
    worker-allocation:
      batch-size: null
      delay: null
```

Then you can use the environment variables `DASK_KUBERNETES__CONTROLLER__WORKER_ALLOCATION__BATCH_SIZE` and `DASK_KUBERNETES__CONTROLLER__WORKER_ALLOCATION__DELAY` in the chart to set them and you can access the values in Python.

```python
import dask.config

...

dask.config.get("kubernetes.controller.worker-allocation.batch-size")
dask.config.get("kubernetes.controller.worker-allocation.delay")
```

(You might find [this utility](https://docs.dask.org/en/latest/configuration.html#conversion-utility) useful for converting between the different formats.)

I'm also keen to let `kopf` help us as much as possible. When batching we effectively want to call the scale workers handler but only scale up by the max batch size, then we want to wait for some period of time and then call the scale workers handler again until we have eventually scaled to our desired number. There is a way to do this by raising exceptions in `kopf`, see my comment below for more information.

These are great improvements, but they don't really change the story with FairScale.

One thing that's missing is synchronization across distributed workers when gathering model and training state, since collecting the state associated with sharded parameters requires a distributed `gather` operation (each worker needs to send its shard of the data to the main process).

Another issue is that the optimizer state actually has to be collected through the `FullyShardedDataParallel` model wrapper ([`gather_full_optim_state`](https://fairscale.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/nn/fsdp.html)).

> Disclaimer: I am not familiar with autoscaler.

I read the PR description, and I have the following questions:

* Q1: What is the step-by-step process for killing a node? The following is my guess.

  * Step 1: **Autoscaler** makes a decision to kill a node based on the information from `GetClusterResourceStat` and `GetInstanceManagerState`.

  * Step 2: **Autoscaler** tells **Node Manager** to kill a node via `AdjustInstances`.

  * Step 3: **Node Manager** kills the node.

  * Step 4: **Autoscaler** knows that the node has already been killed by **Node Manager** by `GetInstanceManagerState`.

  * Step 5: **Cluster Resource Manager** knows that a node is killed by `ReportAutoscalingState`.
    * [update] Cluster Resource Manager can also detect the node loss by itself.

* Q2: Is it possible for Ray to schedule tasks or actors on the killed node between Step 2 and Step 3?

* Q3: Which kinds of information will be included in `ReportAutoscalingState`? 
  * [update] I found the definition in `message ReportAutoscalingStateRequest`.

This looks great so far, the only thing thing is that this would break clusters that use the Autoscaler's built-in `Docker` support.  There is currently a dependency on `/root` being the "home directory" in `def docker_start_cmds(...)` in `docker.py`

I think you can get around this by adding an argument of `home_directory` to `docker_start_cmds`. Then in `command_runner::DockerCommandRunner::run_init`, you can do something like
```
        image_env = self.ssh_command_runner.run(
            "docker inspect -f '{{json .Config.Env}}' " + image,
            with_output=True).decode().strip()
        home_directory = "/root"
        for env_var in json.loads(image_env):
            if env_var.startswith("HOME="):
                home_directory = env_var.split("HOME=")[1]
                break
```

Also, one can include a `label` in the `plot()` call instead of passing it to `legend`, like this:

```python
from photutils import CircularAperture

data = np.ones((50, 50))
ap1 = CircularAperture(((10, 20), (30, 40)), r=3)
ap2 = CircularAperture(((10, 20), (30, 40)), r=5)
ap3 = CircularAperture((30, 20), r=5)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 5))
ax.imshow(data, cmap='Greys')
ap1_patches = ap1.plot(ax, color='red', label='Aper 1')
ap2_patches = ap2.plot(ax, color='blue', label='Aper 2')
ap3_patches = ap3.plot(ax, color='green', label='Aper 3')
handles = (ap1_patches[0], ap2_patches[0], ap3_patches[0])
ax.legend(handles=handles)
```

Thanks for the PR. I've made some review comments to resolve, and then I think we can merge this.

A few requests - 

* please move the .json DebugScripts to ai2thor/unity/debug_scripts or ai2thor/unity/scripts/debug.  Anything in the Unity special folders (https://docs.unity3d.com/Manual/SpecialFolders.html) gets imported as an asset into the asset database.  Unity also watches for any changes to these files, so there is some overhead.  These types of files should live outside the UnityEditor owned directories.

* Instead of using a base path, look at using https://docs.unity3d.com/ScriptReference/EditorUtility.OpenFilePanel.html.  You can then provide a default starting directory for users to select a json file and if they want to load a script from outside the project, they can also do that.  This makes it more convenient if they just want to try something and they have a file living in a different location and don't want to figure out what directory they need to drop the json file in for it to get executed.  As well, this eliminates any chance of typos since they either select a json file or not.

* You are right about Debug.Break.  I used the step frame feature in conjunction with python connected to the editor.  This allowed me to step through actions in python one at a time and view the changes in the editor.  I tried several times to get the Break to trigger before a command got executed, but could not.  I imagine we could come up with some way to get around the behavior but it doesn't seem like all the complexity would improve upon just breaking with a debugger which will actually stop at a breakpoint.  

* The primary reason for using ExecuteBatch + StartCoroutine is that the current way (in the PR) that actions are executed the Unity lifecycle is being skipped for each action.  The default way that debug actions are executed are through the OnEndEdit in the text field. When the action finishes, the frame is rendered and all the steps in the lifecycle are executed (https://docs.unity3d.com/Manual/ExecutionOrder.html).  The most important set of events in the lifecycle for us is the Physics events.  If the actions are executed one after another, the loop is skipped.  A few areas where this matters are when you say pick an object up and drop it.  We have logic that waits for the object to settle by allowing one or more frames to pass and then marking the action finished.  Another is more recently we relied on Unity's built in animator to move the arm.  The way it worked was that it would only calculate the next position of the joints once per frame - so you had to let a frame pass in order for the arm to move.  You don't have to use the same ExecuteBatch function, if you want to create a new one that takes a JArray or List<DynamicServerAction> and does a similar thing that is fine with me.
LG

There are two issues here:
1. The `BuiltPackage` for `GoBinaryFieldSet` `@union` doesn't allow you to have a `GoBuildContext` as an argument of `package_go_binary`: the `package` goal won't know how to initialize one for you. So at each "root" like that, you'll have to initialize it yourself:
```diff
--- a/src/python/pants/backend/go/goals/package_binary.py
+++ b/src/python/pants/backend/go/goals/package_binary.py
@@ -41,8 +41,8 @@ class GoBinaryFieldSet(PackageFieldSet, RunFieldSet):
 @rule(desc="Package Go binary", level=LogLevel.DEBUG)
 async def package_go_binary(
     field_set: GoBinaryFieldSet,
-    go_build_context: GoBuildContext,
 ) -> BuiltPackage:
+    go_build_context = GoBuildContext()
     if field_set.pure.value:
         go_build_context = dataclasses.replace(go_build_context, cgo_allowed=False)

@@ -63,26 +63,24 @@ async def package_go_binary(
```
2. You're explicitly passing the `GoBuildContext` into a bunch of subgraphs where it isn't used: all parameters passed to a `Get` must actually be used in the relevant subgraph:
```diff
@@ -63,13 +63,12 @@ async def package_go_binary(
             "instead of `main`. Go requires that main packages actually use `main` as the package name."
         )

-    build_request = BuildGoPackageTargetRequest(main_pkg.address, is_main=True)
     built_package = await Get(
-        BuiltGoPackage, {build_request: BuildGoPackageTargetRequest, go_build_context: GoBuildContext}
+        BuiltGoPackage, BuildGoPackageTargetRequest(main_pkg.address, is_main=True)
     )
     main_pkg_a_file_path = built_package.import_paths_to_pkg_a_files["main"]
     import_config = await Get(
-        ImportConfig, {ImportConfigRequest(built_package.import_paths_to_pkg_a_files) : ImportConfigRequest, go_build_context: GoBuildContext}
+        ImportConfig, ImportConfigRequest(built_package.import_paths_to_pkg_a_files)
     )
     input_digest = await Get(Digest, MergeDigests([built_package.digest, import_config.digest]))

@@ -82,7 +81,7 @@ async def package_go_binary(
         description=f"Link Go binary for {field_set.address}",
     )
     binary = await Get(
-        LinkedGoBinary, {link_request: LinkGoBinaryRequest, go_build_context: GoBuildContext}
+        LinkedGoBinary, LinkGoBinaryRequest, link_request
     )

     renamed_output_digest = await Get(Digest, AddPrefix(binary.digest, str(output_filename.parent)))
```

----

With those edits things work.

Something to note:

When a process forks we set `TelemetryWriter._forked = True` and prevent [appstarted](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/blob/v1.7.2/ddtrace/internal/telemetry/writer.py#L203), [appclosed](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/blob/v1.7.2/ddtrace/internal/telemetry/writer.py#L228) and [heartbeat](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/blob/v1.7.2/ddtrace/internal/telemetry/writer.py#L215) events from being queued ([link](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/blob/v1.7.2/ddtrace/internal/telemetry/writer.py#L57)). These events should only be sent on the "application" level not on individual processes. 

I am not sure if this impacts the metrics event or if we even need to track metrics sent in different processes. This is just something to keep in mind. @mehulsonowal any thoughts on how we should approach this?

I'm happy to see this making it in. I just need a bit of clarification on some things.

- `eth_utils` has a `ValidationError` that we use in a few places within `web3.py` while in others we use `web3.exceptions.ValidationError`. It looks like at some point there was an idea to use the `eth_utils.exceptions.ValidationError` [across all of the python libraries](https://github.com/ethereum/eth-utils/issues/114). Indeed this isn't used anywhere within the actual `eth-utils` library. Do we want to keep this design? Are there good arguments for or against it? This seems like a good time to revisit this for `web3.py` and it wouldn't be so hard of a change to make. If we think a better design is for each project to have their own `ValidationError` class, do we want to go toward namespace validation errors, as we did with `EthPMValidationError`?

- nit: I slightly prefer the less lengthy `EthPMException` over `PyEthPMException`. The `Py` seems redundant and the module name is `ethpm`. But I can take or leave it. It does make `EthPMValidationError` seem like it needs a `Py` if we keep it... but I prefer both without it.

@jeesang7 - 🤔 Would you be able to try the following?
In a new/fresh virtual environment,
1) `azure-eventhub-checkpointstoreblob> pip install .`
2) `azure-eventhub-checkpointstoreblob> pip install -r dev_requirements.txt`
3) `azure-eventhub-checkpointstoreblob/tests> pytest .`

I think the following might be happening:
When you pip install an editable version of `azure-eventhub-checkpointstoreblob` (i.e. pip install with the `-e` flag), it's looking for `CheckpointStore` inside the `azure/eventhub` folders of the `checkpointstoreblob` package, rather than the `azure-eventhub` package. Basically, it's an issue with pip not being able to find the right namespace. This is a known issue, and we don't have a solution yet. The workaround is to `pip install .` (non-editable) for both `azure-eventhub-checkpointstoreblob` and `azure-eventhub`.
